text: This is a case of a left adrenalectomy. I just want to go over like key steps of the procedure that can make it super, super easy. I kind of learned it from a neurologist, Dr. Alani. So these patients are kind of like completely lateral position, left side up, right side down, obviously.

The surgeon begins mobilizing the descending colon and splenic flexure, ensuring not to go behind the kidney. The mobilization continues until the medial portions of the inferior and superior poles of the kidney are visible.

The surgical field shows the hilum fully exposed, with the adrenal vein located medially. The patient is positioned laterally, causing surrounding tissues to fall away, providing clear access to the adrenal vein.

The surgeon uses two instruments in the left hand and one in the right hand to mobilize tissues superiorly, revealing the left crus of the diaphragm. Minor bleeding is observed from the dissected plane.
The surgeon uses bipolar cautery to carefully flip the spleen, revealing the pancreas along with the splenic vein and splenic artery, requiring cautious handling.
The surgeon performs a medial to lateral mobilization of the colon, ensuring no bleeding is visible while palpating the medial portion of the kidney, which is clearly identifiable.
The surgeon identifies the pancreas, realizing they are in the wrong plane, and plans to adjust. The splenic vein and artery are visible superiorly.

The surgeon begins opening the Gerota's fascia at the level of the hilum, identifying the vein and following it inferiorly. The other hand is used to push the kidney upwards, stretching the surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies the renal artery, which is positioned deeper than the renal vein. The renal vein is visible higher and more superficially in the surgical field.
The surgeon follows the renal vein inferiorly or medially towards the inferior vena cava (IVC), ensuring the superior border is clean to locate the adrenal vein.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to remove surrounding fat tissue after identifying the adrenal vein, without dissecting or clipping the small arterial branches.
The surgeon begins using scissors for finer dissection in the surgical field, focusing on precise tissue separation.
The surgeon clips and transects the renal vein, followed by the dissection and mobilization of the adrenal gland from the kidney, ensuring complete separation of the structures.
The surgeon continues mobilization, revealing the left crus of the diaphragm and the stomach, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon begins the process of separating the adrenal gland from the kidney, using precise dissection techniques to delineate the anatomical boundaries.

The surgeon uses a Vesta sealer to continue the dissection, carefully separating the adrenal gland from the kidney. The kidney becomes visible as the dissection progresses, with no bleeding observed, indicating proper technique.
I'm hoping someone will ask what I clipped up there. Every urologist will know.
The surgeon carefully removes the fiber fatty layer from the kidney and diaphragm, ensuring no bleeding occurs, indicating correct dissection planes.
